text: **Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents**

- **Jinha Chung**
KAIST, Republic of Korea, Daejeon
jinha.chung@kaist.ac.kr

- **Byeongjun Shin**
KAIST, Republic of Korea, Daejeon
byeongjun.shin@kaist.ac.kr

- **Jiin Kim**
KAIST, Republic of Korea, Daejeon
jiin.kim@kaist.ac.kr

- **Minsoo Rhu**
KAIST, Republic of Korea, Daejeon
mrhu@kaist.ac.kr

LLM-based agents deliver state-of-the-art performance across tasks but incur high end-to-end latency on edge devices. We introduce Agent-X, a software-only, accuracy-preserving framework that accelerates both the prefill and decode stages of on-device agent workloads. Agent-X’s two key components rewrite prompts to leverage prefix caching tailored to agent-specific input-token patterns and enable LLM-free speculative decoding for fast token generation with minimal overhead. On representative agentic workloads, Agent-X achieves a 1.61× end-to-end speedup in real systems with no accuracy loss and can be seamlessly integrated into existing on-device AI agents. To the best of our knowledge, ours is the first to systematically characterize and eliminate latency bottlenecks in on-device agents.

The “ChatGPT effect” has taken the world by storm, and Large Language Models (LLMs) are now embedded in various applications that drive our daily lives. LLM-based AI agents elevate the applicability of LLMs through “tool calling.” Equipped with external tools, an LLM can interact with its environment and autonomously execute tasks from start to finish, without further user intervention. As shown in Figure 1, given a user query (1), the AI agent selects the appropriate tools to handle the request (2). The agent then interacts with the environment (e.g., “Contacts”, “Calendar”, and “Email”) by calling the selected tool (3) and reflecting on prior tool output(s) to select the next action (4).

In academia, a rich body of prior work has improved agent task accuracy, while the industry has already started deploying LLM-based agents, boosting user productivity and convenience. On-device AI agents, which run entirely on a user’s local device, provide two unique benefits over cloud-based agents: availability and privacy. On-device agents are always available to the user for immediate use, regardless of the user’s situation (lack of internet access) or the cloud provider’s situation (server outage). Furthermore, growing concerns over data misuse call for measures to guarantee privacy in using LLMs, making on-device agents an attractive solution.

Building on this trend, various hardware and software have been introduced to ease the development and deployment of LLMs at the edge. Despite these advances, on-device AI agents still suffer from suboptimal latency, even for simple tasks.

We analyze the execution of LLM-based agents and identify two LLM instances as primary bottlenecks. Our analysis shows that, unlike conventional server-based, conversation-oriented applications where latency is dominated by decoding, both the prefill and decode stages contribute significantly to end-to-end latency in on-device environments due to the agentic workflow and hardware constraints. We further characterize these stages at the token level and observe two key properties.

1. During prefill, the prompt structure limits the applicability of optimizations such as prefix caching.
2. During decode, the output is largely grounded in few-shot examples and does not fully exploit the LLM’s reasoning capability.

Building on our characterization, we propose Agent-X, which combines PromptWeaver and ExSpec to accelerate the prefill and decode stages of on-device agentic LLMs, respectively. PromptWeaver dynamically reconstructs the input prompt to enable efficient prefix caching.

| Task                         | Status                          |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Schedule a meeting with John | Meeting scheduled successfully! |
| Retrieved John’s email       | <john@mobisys.com>              |
| Created calendar event       | Invited <john@mobisys.com>      |
| Task executed successfully!  |                                 |

| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------- |

text:
- Jinha Chung
- Byeongjun Shin
- Jiin Kim
- Minsoo Rhu

- **System prompt**: Given a user query, create a plan to solve it with …
- **Planner (LLM)**: Decision guidelines: You must only output Finish or Replan …
- **Tool descriptions**:
- 1. `get_email_address(name: str) -> str`: Search for a contact by name.
- 2. `create_calendar_event(title: str, start_date: str, ...)`: The default meeting duration is 60 minutes.

- Example question: Set up a meeting at 2PM today and invite Sarah.
1. `get_email_address("Sarah")`
2. `create_calendar_event("Meeting", "2025-01-01 14:00:00", "2025-01-01 15:00:00", "", ["$1"], "", None)`
3. `finish()`
- Schedule a meeting with John tomorrow at 5pm.
- Description: The whole pipeline consists of two LLMs (Planner and Arbiter) and a series of tool calls (carried out by Execution unit) to fully serve the user query (blue).

- **Agentic workflows**: LLM-based agents enhance LLMs’ capabilities by interacting with the external environment. The means of interaction are referred to as tools, which are APIs available within the system.
- **On-device LLMs**: AI functionalities are now available on edge devices, appearing in various forms like voice transcription, Circle-to-Search, and personal assistants.
- On-device agents make use of on-device LLMs to power local agents executing entirely on the user’s device.

| Tool Name                      | Description                                     |
| ------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- |
| `get_email_address(name: str)` | Search for a contact by name.                   |
| `create_calendar_event(...)`   | Create a calendar event with specified details. |

Create a calendar event with specified details.

- **Planner Output**:

- **Figure 8**: Tool co-activation heatmap of a subset of tools in TinyAgent training dataset.
- **Figure 9**: Input and output example of Planner for the query “Schedule a meeting with John tomorrow at 5pm.”

- **Planner Input Tokens**: The Planner input consists of the static system prompt, retrieved tool descriptions and guidelines, and retrieved tool-use examples (see Figure 2).
- **Token Count Distribution**: Figure 7 shows the average token-count distribution of Planner inputs.
- **Arbiter Input Tokens**: The Arbiter’s input includes decision guidelines and examples, along with the list of call-observation pairs produced by tool execution (see Figure 2).

- **Planner and Arbiter Output Tokens**: Figure 9 shows an example tool-use example alongside the output for the query “Schedule a meeting with John tomorrow at 5pm.”
- **Token Overlap**: Empirically, 96% of Planner and 87% of Arbiter output tokens overlap with those in their corresponding input prompt.

- **Definition**: The likelihood of certain tools being called together across different queries.
- **Example**: get_zoom_meeting_link is more likely to be co-activated with get_email_address (91%) or compose_new_email (58%).

- **Draft Model Selection**: Speculative decoding requires a carefully chosen draft model for high efficiency.
- **Performance Trade-offs**: A smaller draft model may yield little performance improvement, while a larger model introduces substantial latency overhead.
- **Table 2**: Theoretical max. speedup achievable by each draft model under the Planner workload.

| get\_email\_address     | Email\_A     | Q. Open the note 'Plan'              | Cluster 1     |
| compose\_new\_email     | Email\_B     | • Email\_A and send it to Sarah …    | Cluster 2     |
| open\_note              | Note\_A      | • open\_note('Plan') …               | Cluster 1     |
| add\_note\_content      | Note\_B      | • open\_note('Tasks') …              | Cluster 3     |
| create\_calendar\_event | Calendar     | Q. Open 'Tasks' and add 'Buy milk' … | Cluster 3     |

text:
- **Ground Truth Tools Used:**
- get_email_address(…)
- compose_new_email(…)
- open_note(…)
- **Tool Co-activation Matrix**: An adjacency matrix where each graph node corresponds to a tool and edges denote the number of times the corresponding pair of tools has been called together.
- The KV cache enables static prompts to be precomputed and stored in SSD, significantly reducing the number of uncacheable tokens in the prompt.
- The remaining uncacheable input tokens primarily consist of tool-use examples, which are selected at runtime based on the user prompt.
- Clusters must be given a fixed ordering to maximize KV cache reuse.
- Tools are clustered based on their co-activation locality, utilizing themes related to the tool’s usage.

- **Figure 12**: Illustration of PromptWeaver’s offline KV cache precompute mechanism.
- **Figure 8**: Co-activation locality-based tool clustering.
- The performance benefits of the approach significantly outweigh the overhead introduced by a larger KV cache, enhancing the efficiency of on-device AI agents.

- **Figure 12**: Illustration of PromptWeaver’s offline KV cache precompute mechanism.
- **Figure 8**: Co-activation locality-based tool clustering.

text:
- Jinha Chung
- Byeongjun Shin
- Jiin Kim

- **Input**: KV cache budget, Planner’s training dataset D
- **Output**: Cluster combination KV cache C
- **Initialization**:
- C ← ∅
- prefixes ← {all prefixes of sequences in D}
- **Process**:
1. For i = 1 to n do
- options ← ∅
- For all prefix in prefixes do
- If len(prefix) == 1 or prefix[:-1] in C then
- options.add(prefix)
- End if
- End for
- ˆ = arg max [ coverage(D, C ∪ {prefix}) - coverage(D, C) ] for prefix in options
- C.add(ˆ)
2. End for
- **Return**: C
Section 4.3: ExSpec - Example-based Selective Speculative Decoding
section_path: Section 4.3: ExSpec - Example-based Selective Speculative Decoding

text:
- **Overview**: Discusses the predictability of Planner and Arbiter output tokens and the inefficiencies of naive speculative decoding.
- **Mechanisms**:
1. Lightweight draft token generation using an n-gram model.
2. Selective fallback to autoregressive generation when speculative decoding is inefficient.

- The total number of possible cluster combinations is \(2^n - 1\) for n ordered clusters.
- PromptWeaver maximizes KV cache reuse while minimizing SSD capacity overhead.
- ExSpec aims to remain lightweight while ensuring outputs are closely correlated with input prompts.
- A simple n-gram lookup table (LUT) is built on-the-fly for each user query, populated with few-shot examples.

- **Table of Tool-use Examples**:
- Key: get_email
- Value: get_email_address
- Examples:
1. get_email_address("Sarah")
2. create_calendar_event(…)
3. finish()

The dataset [68] serves three roles:
- PromptWeaver uses the training split for tool clustering and combination selection.
- The test split is used for evaluation.
- Before each task, we flush the system’s page cache to isolate the cost of loading the KV cache from storage.

text: Experiments run on an Apple Mac mini with an M4 Pro chip [12], 64 GB of memory, 512 GB of SSD storage, 12 CPU cores, and 16 GPU cores. We build PromptWeaver and ExSpec on MLX-LM [10] and MLX-engine [43]. MLX-LM is Apple Silicon’s official LLM inference package, backed by MLX [33]. MLX-engine is an open-source MLX LLM engine from LM Studio. The software stack comprises MLX v0.25.2, a modified MLX-LM v0.25.1, and MLX-engine commit #ecc2cf4 on macOS Sequoia 15.5.

text: Balancing is crucial for maximizing ExSpec’s performance. With a small context size, for example, a unigram model (k = 1), the LUT always proposes the single most frequent token from the few-shot examples and user query, yielding low-quality draft tokens. Conversely, a large context size provides richer context and higher-quality drafts but often fails to predict unseen sequences, defaulting to random tokens when the (k - 1) prefix is not available in the LUT. We find that a trigram model (k = 3) offers the best trade-off between draft quality and LUT hit rate. The impact of k is further explored in Section 5.5.

text: In line with prior work [19, 36], we define the Planner accuracy by constructing a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) from the output plan, where each node represents a function call and a directed edge represents the dependency. We compare the generated DAG against the ground truth’s DAG. Because the tool calls are deterministic, the Planner accuracy directly translates to the end-to-end task accuracy of the agent. PromptWeaver adds k (0 ≤ k ≤ 4) tool-use examples to make up for its accuracy loss.
section_path: Document Title: Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents
Document Title: Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents

text: Compares the Planner’s task accuracy changes with the baseline as more examples are added. Not surprisingly, k = 0 results in lower accuracy (0.832), lower than baseline’s 0.836. We observe that the accuracy peaks at k = 1 (0.841) and falls as more examples are added.
section_path: Document Title: Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents
Document Title: Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents

text: Shows how the share of cacheable vs. uncacheable tokens changes as tool-use examples are added. Each new example is uncacheable, so the uncacheable fraction grows from 11% at k=0 to 21% at k=4. Accuracy peaks at k=1, so we adopt this setting for PromptWeaver. With k=1, PromptWeaver averages 519 uncacheable tokens, a 70% drop from the baseline’s 1,711. This result highlights PromptWeaver’s ability to minimize uncacheable tokens.

text: Precomputed KV caches are stored in SSD and loaded to memory on demand to reduce online prefill compute. Because the agentic workloads display long prompt lengths, the size of the KV cache is also large. Expanding the cluster budget boosts tool-use example coverage (Section 4.2) but also raises storage cost, as shown in Figure 15. At budget 0, only static tokens are cached, using 0.95 GB (0.57 GB, Planner + 0.39 GB, Arbiter). Coverage grows with larger budgets but levels off beyond 15 clusters. We therefore fix the budget at 15 clusters for the remainder of the evaluation, using 6.26 GB of storage for 74.4% coverage.

text:
- Jinha Chung
- Byeongjun Shin
- Jiin Kim
- Minsoo Rhu

- **End-to-end speedup.**
- Figure 18 shows the latency and speedup of end-to-end on-device agentic workloads. The impacts of PromptWeaver (PW), ExSpec (ES), and Agent-X (PW+ES) are shown. Applying PromptWeaver and ExSpec independently provides end-to-end speedups of 1.16× and 1.43×, respectively. Applying PromptWeaver and ExSpec together reaps an end-to-end speedup of 1.61×. Alongside the speedup, it is worth noting that Agent-X is a purely software solution that can be directly applied to existing on-device agentic systems without degrading the accuracy of the agent.

- **Speedup analysis.**
- Figure 17 reports the decode stage latency of speculative decoding with draft LLM Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct. SpecDec experiences slowdown rather than speedup over the baseline. Other than the multi-token tax, the overhead that comes from dealing with different tokenizers between the target and the draft model further slows down the system to achieve a much slower speed than was expected from Table 2. On the contrary, the non-selective design of ExSpec effectively avoids any draft LLM-related overheads, speeding up the decode stage by 1.38×. With selective decoding, ExSpec can avoid the multi-token tax when no draft tokens are accepted, falling back to autoregressive generation 17 (Planner) and 37 (Arbiter) times per query. Overall, ExSpec reduces the decode latency by 1.73×, establishing itself as a lightweight and performant solution.

- **Draft token accuracy.**
- Table 3 compares draft-token accuracy under selective and non-selective ExSpec. Although non-selective decoding produces many more draft tokens, both modes accept their efficacy on smaller LLMs.

- **Figure 16:** Prefill stage latency (left) and speedup gain (right) of PromptWeaver.
- **Figure 17:** Decode stage latency (left) and speedup (right) of speculative decoding (SpecDec) and ExSpec.
- **Figure 18:** End-to-end latency (left) and speedup (right) of Agent-X. PW denotes PromptWeaver, ES denotes ExSpec, and PW+ES denotes the full Agent-X pipeline.

- **Table 3:** Draft token accuracy comparison between non-selective and selective decoding schemes of ExSpec.
- | Workflow component | Applied method | Generated draft tokens | Accepted draft tokens | Accuracy |
- The impact of PromptWeaver on Planner and Arbiter prefill stages is quantified, showing various speedup metrics and reductions in uncached input tokens.

- Application to other platforms, agents, and models.
- As PromptWeaver and ExSpec are both purely software solutions, Agent-X can easily be ported to other hardware platforms. The core algorithms of Agent-X are hardware-agnostic and applicable to other platforms.

- The document discusses the performance and integration of various components in a system designed for on-device agentic workloads, highlighting the efficiency of the proposed methods and their applicability across different platforms.
